alloy_matches.reason: Same alloy name, base element, alloying elements, and nominal composition (prediction uses rounded values, truth uses measured, but semantically identical).

process_matches.reason: Prediction adds vacuum induction melting step but otherwise identical homogenization, hot rolling, solution treatment and quenching; core process is the same.

sample_matches.reason: Both samples reference the same alloy and process, and context_text describes identical microstructure (97% gamma, 3% alpha, 10.8 μm grain size, 1.6e13 dislocations).

truth.papers.A50.title: Excellent mechanical properties and underlined deformation twinning mechanism of a low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel
truth.papers.A50.paper_scope: Investigates mechanical properties and deformation twinning mechanism of Fe-35Mn-6Al-0.1C low-density steel at room and cryogenic temperatures, highlighting the roles of short-range ordering and planar slip in promoting twinning despite high stacking fault energy.

prediction.papers.A50.title: Excellent mechanical properties and underlined deformation twinning mechanism of a low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel
prediction.papers.A50.paper_scope: Investigation of mechanical properties, cryogenic toughness, and deformation twinning mechanisms in Fe-35Mn-6Al-0.1C low-carbon low-density steel at room temperature and liquid nitrogen temperature.

truth.alloys.alloy_fe35mn6al01c.alloys_notes: Measured composition from wet-chemical analysis (specimen). Also contains microalloying elements Nb, V, Mo, W.

prediction.alloys.alloy_fe35mn6al01c.alloys_notes: Measured density is 7.18 g·cm⁻³. Stacking fault energy (SFE) is 62.64 mJ·m⁻² at room temperature and 49.46 mJ·m⁻² at liquid nitrogen temperature. Measured composition of specimen: Mn 35.14, Al 5.97, C 0.095, Nb 0.11, V 0.10, Mo 1.24, W 0.95, Fe Bal. (wt.%). Austenite phase composition: Mn 35.92, Al 5.88, C 0.098, Nb 0.09, V 0.09, Mo 1.12, W 0.86, Fe Bal. Ferrite phase composition: Mn 32.44, Al 6.83, C -, Nb 0.09, V 0.18, Mo 1.54, W 1.63, Fe Bal.

truth.processes.proc_fe35mn6al01c_01.descripti: Homogenized at 1150 °C for 4 h, hot-rolled to rods of 16.5 mm diameter, solution-treated at 1050 °C for 2 h followed by oil quenching.

prediction.processes.proc_fe35mn6al01c_01.descripti: Vacuum induction melting of 50 Kg ingot under high purity Ar atmosphere, homogenization at 1150 °C for 4 h, hot-rolling into rods with 16.5 mm diameter, and solution treatment at 1050 °C for 2 h followed by oil quenching.

oil quenching to obtain equiaxed grain structures

Oil quenching to obtain equiaxed grain structures with nearly equivalent grain sizes.

truth.structures.sample_fe35mn6al01c_01.overal: Initial austenitic matrix with ~3% ferrite at grain boundaries; after deformation, development of dislocation substructures and deformation twinning, strongly dependent on temperature and strain.
truth.structures.sample_fe35mn6al01c_01.micros: equiaxed grains with annealing twin boundaries
partially dissolved Nb, Mo, W; V fully dissolved; approx. 0.047% Nb, 0.017% Mo, 0.024% W in MC
detected by extremely weak superlattice spots in SAD pattern
deformed, containing dislocation substructures and twins
first observed at ~28% strain at RT
deformed, early twinning at annealed twin boundaries
first observed at ~10% strain at LNT, closely related to prior stacking faults
throughout grains; secondary twins contain more stacking faults
extensive activation of primary and secondary twins at 55% strain
low temperature promotes twin formation, but twins do not burst as in TWIP steels

prediction.structures.sample_fe35mn6al01c_01.overal: Austenite-based duplex structure with FCC γ matrix embedded with a small fraction of BCC α phase
prediction.structures.sample_fe35mn6al01c_01.micros: equiaxed grains with annealing twin boundaries
uniformly distributed along austenite grain boundaries
extremely weak superlattice spots in SAED along [100]γ
Approximately 0.047 wt.% Nb, 0.017 wt.% Mo, and 0.024 wt.% W exist in MC carbide; V is completely dissolved.

truth.interfaces.sample_fe35mn6al01c_01.defect: γ/α interfaces serve as crack initiation sites during cryogenic impact, contributing to decrease in crack propagation energy.
truth.interfaces.sample_fe35mn6al01c_01.interf: Stress concentration at γ/α interfaces may lead to void nucleation and cracking at LNT.
truth.interfaces.sample_fe35mn6al01c_01.phase_: Austenite remains stable during deformation at RT and LNT; no martensitic transformation observed.

prediction.interfaces.sample_fe35mn6al01c_01.interf: Under cryogenic impact, distinct fine cracks appear around voids generated by the detachment of α phase, indicating the γ/α interface serves as a crack initiation site.
prediction.interfaces.sample_fe35mn6al01c_01.phase_: The small fraction of ferrite is stable during deformation at both room temperature and liquid nitrogen temperature; no phase transformation occurs in austenite under impact.

prediction.properties.sample_fe35mn6al01c_01.mechan: Engineering stress-strain curve and true stress-strain with strain hardening rate curve are shown in Fig. 2(a) and Fig. 2(b); strain hardening exhibits four stages (A, B, C, D) at liquid nitrogen temperature.
four-stage hardening behavior (stages A, B, C, D)

truth.performance.sample_fe35mn6al01c_01.compa: Total elongation ~3 times higher at LNT, lower cost due to simple heat treatment and low-cost alloying

prediction.performance.sample_fe35mn6al01c_01.compa: 9Ni steel, 304 stainless steel, TWIP steel, Al0.1CoCrFeNi high-entropy alloy, high-carbon lightweight steel, low-carbon high manganese steel

truth.characterization_methods.sample_fe35mn6a: Evaluate impact toughness and separate crack initiation/propagation energy
331 J at RT, 254 J at LNT; crack initiation energy ~80–100 J constant, crack propagation energy significantly lower at LNT.

prediction.characterization_methods.sample_fe35mn6a: Measure impact toughness and record force-displacement and energy-displacement curves at RT and LNT

Standard samples 55×10×10 mm with 2 mm V-notch. CVN energy is 331 ± 4 J at RT and 254 ± 6 J at LNT. Crack initiation energy is ~80–100 J for both temperatures; crack propagation energy is 251 J at RT and 156 J at LNT. Fracture surfaces are ductile (dimples) at both temperatures.

truth.computational_details.sample_fe35mn6al01: Calculated SFE values consistent with observed twinning tendency despite high SFE; phase diagram matches XRD phase stability.
Calculate SFE of austenite based on thermodynamic Gibbs energy
ρ (molar surface density), ΔG^{γ→ε} from thermodynamic functions and mixing energies, interfacial energy σ; used magnetic contribution model.
SFE = 62.64 mJ/m² at RT, 49.46 mJ/m² at LNT. Calculated according to approach in [52,53].
Estimate critical stress for deformation twinning
Taylor factor M (2.2 Sachs, 3.06 Taylor, 2.7 CPFE), SFE Γ, shear modulus G=68 GPa, Burgers vector b=0.147 nm, grain size D=10.8 μm
Experimental twinning stress ~723 MPa at RT, ~778 MPa at LNT; Sachs model gives closest prediction at LNT.
m=0.326 (average Schmid factor), b=0.25 nm, γ=SFE, K=0.350 MPa·m^0.5
Critical twinning stress from HP-type relation: 1096 MPa RT, 934 MPa LNT.

prediction.computational_details.sample_fe35mn6al01: Thermodynamic calculation of stacking fault energy and phase diagram
XRD profiles and measured phase fractions validate the predicted austenite stability from phase diagram calculation.
γ_SFE at RT: 62.64 mJ·m⁻²; at LNT: 49.46 mJ·m⁻². ΔG^(γ→ε) at RT: 804.22 J·mol⁻¹; at LNT: 576.90 J·mol⁻¹.
Based on molar Gibbs energy of γ→ε transformation and interfacial energy; solid solution model with magnetic transformation Gibbs energy included.
Estimate critical stress for twinning nucleation
σ_t = M(Γ/b + Gb/D); M values: 2.2 (Sachs), 2.7 (CPFEM), 3.06 (Taylor random), 3.64 (<111>//TA and <110>//TA); G=68 GPa; b=0.147 nm; D=10.8 μm; Sfe RT=62.64, LNT=49.46 mJ·m⁻².
Experimental critical twinning stress is about 723 MPa at RT and 778 MPa at LNT. Sachs model predicts 939 MPa (RT) and 742 MPa (LNT); Taylor model predicts 1306 MPa (RT) and 1032 MPa (LNT).
Estimate critical twinning stress via Hall-Petch-type relation
Predicted 1096 MPa at RT and 934 MPa at LNT. K_GB(γ)=0.350 MPa·m^0.5 estimated from yield strengths at different solution treatment temperatures.
Predict flow stress contributions from lattice friction, grain boundaries, twins, and dislocations

σ_mod = 0.97σ_mod(γ) + 0.03σ_mod(α); α=0.4 for austenite (verified as best fit); M=3.06; G=68 GPa; b=0.26 nm. Lattice friction: 143 MPa (RT), 389 MPa (LNT). Grain boundary strengthening: ~108 MPa. Twin parameters at 55 % LNT: l=182 nm, e=72 nm, F from stereological analysis, twin strengthening=290 MPa.
Dislocation hardening dominates at RT; synergistic dislocation and twinning hardening at LNT. Ferrite contribution <10 MPa and neglected.

truth.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: Deformation twinning is observed at RT despite high SFE (62.64 mJ/m²), attributed to stress concentration from planar dislocation substructures and short-range ordering (SRO) effect that may lower effective SFE.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: At LNT, crack initiation energy remains ~80–100 J similar to RT, while crack propagation energy drops from ~251 J at RT to ~156 J at LNT, leading to lower total impact energy (254 J vs 331 J).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: The presence of ~3% ferrite refines austenite grains and enhances toughness, but γ/α interfaces become crack initiation sites at LNT, somewhat reducing crack propagation energy.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: The work hardening at LNT exhibits four stages: A (dislocation accumulation/recovery), B (nearly constant hardening due to twin nucleation at annealed twin boundaries), C (decreasing hardening from dislocation substructure refinement), D (sustained hardening from extensive primary and secondary twinning).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: The density of the steel is 7.18 g/cm³, contributing to lightweight advantage over conventional cryogenic steels.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Fig.10 compares Charpy impact energy and specific yield strength, showing this steel has higher impact energy than high-carbon lightweight steel and TWIP steel, and superior specific yield strength to 304 stainless steel and low-carbon high-Mn steel.

prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: Table 2 reports reduction of area (RA): 78 ± 0.5 % at room temperature and 80 ± 1 % at liquid nitrogen temperature for the solution-treated steel.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Table 3 gives true stress, geometrically necessary dislocation density (ρ^GND from EBSD), XRD dislocation density (ρ), and cell block/dislocation cell (CBs/DCs) sizes at engineering strains of 3 %, 10 %, 20 %, 28 %, 38 %, and 55 % at both RT and LNT. At RT, true stress increases from 339 MPa (3 %) to 800 MPa (38 %); ρ^GND (RT) from 0.71×10¹⁴ to 8.99×10¹⁴ m⁻²; ρ (XRD, RT) from 0.64×10¹⁴ to 8.03×10¹⁴ m⁻². At LNT, true stress increases from 598 MPa (3 %) to 1463 MPa (55 %); ρ^GND (LNT) from 0.97×10¹⁴ to 9.73×10¹⁴ m⁻²; ρ (XRD, LNT) from 0.72×10¹⁴ to 13.32×10¹⁴ m⁻². CBs or DCs size at 20 %: 448 ± 74 nm (RT) and 429 ± 76 nm (LNT); at 28 %: 282 ± 56 nm (RT) and 242 ± 54 nm (LNT).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Fig. 2(b) shows true stress-strain and strain hardening rate curves: RT exhibits two-stage hardening, while LNT exhibits four-stage hardening (stages A, B, C, D). Stage B at LNT is associated with twin activation at annealed twin boundaries; Stage D involves extensive primary and secondary twin activation sustaining high strain hardening.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Fig. 4 data: Average {111}<110>γ Schmid factor at RT peaks near 3 % strain (~0.438 initial, ~0.424 at 3 %), while {111}<112>γ Schmid factor at RT decreases then surpasses slip Schmid factor. At LNT, both {111}<110>γ and {111}<112>γ Schmid factors peak at approximately 10 % engineering strain.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: Fig. 5 and Fig. 7–8 descriptions indicate strong orientation dependence of deformation twinning: type I grains (<001>//TA, deviation ~15°) form dislocation cells and show few twins; type II grains (between <001> and <111>) form single-slip traces and single-orientation twins; type III grains (<111>//TA, deviation ~15°) form double glide traces and multiple-orientation twins. Twins at RT initiate at ~28 % strain along cell block walls; at LNT twins initiate at ~10 % strain at annealed twin boundaries with twin bundle width ~5 nm.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Fig. 6 indicates that at 28 % strain, type II grains exhibit single slip traces and type III grains exhibit double glide traces; grain subdivision is induced by dislocation glide.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: Fig. 9 indicates impact fracture surfaces at both RT and LNT are ductile (micrometer or sub-micrometer dimples). Cryogenic impact sample shows distinct fine cracks around voids generated by α phase detachment, confirming γ/α interface as crack initiation site and contributing to reduced crack propagation energy at low temperature.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: Fig. 10 benchmark data: The studied steel exhibits Charpy impact energy ~254 J and specific yield strength ~70 MPa·cm³·g⁻¹ at LNT, offering a higher Charpy impact energy than high-carbon lightweight steel and TWIP steel, and superior specific yield strength than low-carbon high manganese steel and 304 stainless steel; ductility is nearly 3 times higher than 9Ni steel.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_8.value: Fig. 11 XRD profiles and phase diagram (Thermal-Calc TCFE12) confirm austenite stability: about 95.6–97.3 % austenite retained after LNT tensile strains; ferrite fraction remains small and stable.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_9.value: Strengthening contributions from Table 6: At 55 % LNT engineering strain, austenite dislocation strengthening σ_dis(γ)=646 MPa and twin strengthening σ_twin(γ)=290 MPa. Ferrite dislocation contribution <10 MPa and neglected in flow stress modeling.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_10.value: Williamson–Hall equation and Gaussian curve fitting applied to XRD profiles of (111)γ, (200)γ, and (220)γ peaks to estimate crystallite size D and micro-strain for dislocation density calculation via Eq. (2) with Burgers vector b=0.26 nm.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_11.value: Geometrically necessary dislocation density estimated from EBSD KAM data using Eq. (1): ρ^GND = 2θ/ub, where u is unit length and b is Burgers vector.
